source_paper_title: Fair and Efficient Multi-resource Allocation for Cloud Computing: Beyond Dominant Resource Fairness
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bei_2026, title={Fair and Efficient Multi-resource Allocation for Cloud Computing: Beyond Dominant Resource Fairness}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0714}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0714}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Bei, Xiaohui and Li, Zihao and Luo, Junjie}, year={2026}, month=feb }
context:
Section 5 (Price of Strategyproofness), page 30 (discussion after Theorem 7) and page 33 (statement preceding Lemma 16).

For the case when \(m = 3\), we show that the price of SP is still \(\infty\) for utilization, while for SW we can only get a lower bound of 2.
We leave the gap between 2 and 3 as an open question.
title: Determine the price of strategyproofness for social welfare with three resources
background_latex:
Consider the multi-resource allocation model with a set of agents \(N=\{1,2,\dots,n\}\) and a set of divisible resources \(R=\{1,2,\dots,m\}\), where each resource has total supply normalized to \(1\).

Each agent \(i\in N\) has a normalized demand vector \(d_i=(d_{i1},\dots,d_{im})\in(0,1]^m\) with \(\max_{r\in R} d_{ir}=1\). The dominant resource of agent \(i\) is any \(r_i^*\in\arg\max_{r\in R} d_{ir}\).

An allocation is a matrix \(A=(A_{ir})_{i\in N,r\in R}\) where \(A_{ir}\ge 0\) is the fraction of resource \(r\) allocated to agent \(i\). Feasibility requires \(\sum_{i\in N} A_{ir}\le 1\) for all \(r\in R\).

Agents have Leontief preferences: the utility of agent \(i\) under allocation \(A\) is
\[
 u_i(A_i)=\max\{y\ge 0: A_{ir}\ge y\, d_{ir}\ \text{for all } r\in R\},
\]
where \(A_i=(A_{i1},\dots,A_{im})\).

A mechanism is a function \(f\) mapping every demand profile \((d_1,\dots,d_n)\) to a feasible allocation \(f(d_1,\dots,d_n)\).

Define the following properties for a mechanism \(f\):

1) Share incentive (SI): for every instance, for all \(i\), \(u_i(f_i)\ge 1/n\).

2) Envy freeness (EF): for every instance, for all \(i,j\), \(u_i(f_i)\ge u_i(f_j)\).

3) Pareto optimality (PO): for every instance, there is no feasible allocation \(A'\) with \(u_i(A'_i)\ge u_i(f_i)\) for all \(i\) and \(u_{i_0}(A'_{i_0})>u_{i_0}(f_{i_0})\) for some \(i_0\).

4) Strategyproofness (SP): for every instance and agent \(i\), misreporting \(d'_i\) cannot increase \(u_i\), i.e., if \(I\) is the true profile and \(I'\) differs only in agent \(i\)'s report, then \(u_i(f_i(I))\ge u_i(f_i(I'))\).

The utilitarian social welfare of an allocation is \(\mathrm{SW}(A)=\sum_{i\in N} u_i(A_i)\).

Given an instance \(I=(d_1,\dots,d_n)\), let \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)\) denote the maximum social welfare over all feasible allocations that satisfy SI and EF:
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)=\max\{\mathrm{SW}(A): A \text{ feasible and satisfies SI and EF}\}.
\]

For a mechanism \(f\), its fair-ratio for social welfare is
\[
\mathrm{FR}_{\mathrm{SW}}(f)=\sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(I))}.
\]

For a fixed number of resources \(m\), the price of strategyproofness (for social welfare) is the best achievable fair-ratio among mechanisms satisfying SI, EF, PO, and SP:
\[
\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(m)=\inf_{f\ \text{satisfies SI,EF,PO,SP}} \mathrm{FR}_{\mathrm{SW}}(f).
\]

The setting of interest here is \(m=3\) resources.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the exact value of \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)\), where
\[
\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)=\inf_{f\ \text{satisfies SI,EF,PO,SP}}\ \sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(I))}
\]
and \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)=\max\{\mathrm{SW}(A): A \text{ feasible and satisfies SI and EF}\}\) for the 3-resource Leontief multi-resource allocation problem described above.

Equivalently, close (or determine) the gap between the known bounds
\[
2 \le \mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3) \le 3.
\]
latex:
Consider the multi-resource allocation model with a set of agents \(N=\{1,2,\dots,n\}\) and a set of divisible resources \(R=\{1,2,\dots,m\}\), where each resource has total supply normalized to \(1\).

Each agent \(i\in N\) has a normalized demand vector \(d_i=(d_{i1},\dots,d_{im})\in(0,1]^m\) with \(\max_{r\in R} d_{ir}=1\). The dominant resource of agent \(i\) is any \(r_i^*\in\arg\max_{r\in R} d_{ir}\).

An allocation is a matrix \(A=(A_{ir})_{i\in N,r\in R}\) where \(A_{ir}\ge 0\) is the fraction of resource \(r\) allocated to agent \(i\). Feasibility requires \(\sum_{i\in N} A_{ir}\le 1\) for all \(r\in R\).

Agents have Leontief preferences: the utility of agent \(i\) under allocation \(A\) is
\[

1) Share incentive (SI): for every instance, for all \(i\), \(u_i(f_i)\ge 1/n\).

2) Envy freeness (EF): for every instance, for all \(i,j\), \(u_i(f_i)\ge u_i(f_j)\).

3) Pareto optimality (PO): for every instance, there is no feasible allocation \(A'\) with \(u_i(A'_i)\ge u_i(f_i)\) for all \(i\) and \(u_{i_0}(A'_{i_0})>u_{i_0}(f_{i_0})\) for some \(i_0\).

4) Strategyproofness (SP): for every instance and agent \(i\), misreporting \(d'_i\) cannot increase \(u_i\), i.e., if \(I\) is the true profile and \(I'\) differs only in agent \(i\)'s report, then \(u_i(f_i(I))\ge u_i(f_i(I'))\).

The utilitarian social welfare of an allocation is \(\mathrm{SW}(A)=\sum_{i\in N} u_i(A_i)\).

Given an instance \(I=(d_1,\dots,d_n)\), let \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)\) denote the maximum social welfare over all feasible allocations that satisfy SI and EF:
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)=\max\{\mathrm{SW}(A): A \text{ feasible and satisfies SI and EF}\}.
\]

For a mechanism \(f\), its fair-ratio for social welfare is
\[
\mathrm{FR}_{\mathrm{SW}}(f)=\sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(I))}.
\]

For a fixed number of resources \(m\), the price of strategyproofness (for social welfare) is the best achievable fair-ratio among mechanisms satisfying SI, EF, PO, and SP:
\[
\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(m)=\inf_{f\ \text{satisfies SI,EF,PO,SP}} \mathrm{FR}_{\mathrm{SW}}(f).
\]

The setting of interest here is \(m=3\) resources.

Determine the exact value of \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)\), where
\[
\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)=\inf_{f\ \text{satisfies SI,EF,PO,SP}}\ \sup_I \frac{\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(I))}
\]
and \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)=\max\{\mathrm{SW}(A): A \text{ feasible and satisfies SI and EF}\}\) for the 3-resource Leontief multi-resource allocation problem described above.

Equivalently, close (or determine) the gap between the known bounds
\[
2 \le \mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3) \le 3.
\]
location_in_paper: Section 5 (Price of Strategyproofness), page 30 (discussion after Theorem 7) and page 33 (statement preceding Lemma 16).
open_problem_quote:
For the case when \(m = 3\), we show that the price of SP is still \(\infty\) for utilization, while for SW we can only get a lower bound of 2.
We leave the gap between 2 and 3 as an open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a tight worst-case bound over all instances and all SI/EF/PO/SP mechanisms, which is not a finite search space. However, automated search could plausibly discover hard instances that push candidate mechanisms toward lower bounds, or suggest conjectured tight values by optimizing over small discretized demand profiles and mechanisms from parameterized families.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate mechanisms via parameterized rules (e.g., variants of DRF/UNB/BAL-style water-filling with tunable priorities) and evaluate them on large sets of sampled/discretized 3-resource instances using computed \(\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{fair}}(I)\) (via LP) to estimate \(\sup_I\) approximately. This gives a usable fitness signal, though proving optimality or finding the true supremum would still require theory beyond the search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Bei–Li–Luo) reframes welfare guarantees for DRF-like mechanisms via the fair-ratio benchmark, comparing a mechanism’s social welfare to the best social welfare achievable among allocations that already satisfy the hard fairness constraints SI and EF. Within this benchmark they define the price of strategyproofness \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(m)\), i.e., the best worst-case fair-ratio achievable by mechanisms satisfying SI, EF, PO, and SP. For \(m=3\) they prove only coarse bounds \(2 \le \mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3) \le 3\), leaving the exact value open.

Technically, their lower-bound approach adapts the classic framework of Parkes–Procaccia–Shah (which established strong welfare limitations under truthfulness/fairness for DRF-type settings) but must additionally respect SI and EF in the benchmark optimum. For \(m\ge 4\) they show the price equals \(m\), while for \(m=3\) the construction yields only a factor-2 lower bound. No forward-citation evidence was provided here, so there is currently no identified subsequent paper closing the gap to 3 or proving tightness at 2.

Thus, based on the available record, determining the exact \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)\) remains open. Promising directions include either (i) strengthening the lower bound beyond 2 by refining the envy-freeness-preserving adversarial instances used in the \(m\ge 4\) case to work with only three resources, or (ii) designing an explicit SI+EF+PO+SP mechanism with fair-ratio strictly below 3 (ideally matching a new lower bound), potentially by extending the two-resource hybrid ideas (switching between different truthful fair mechanisms based on instance structure) to three resources.

sources.title: Fair and Efficient Multi-Resource Allocation for Cloud Computing: Beyond Dominant Resource Fairness
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the fair-ratio benchmark \,\(\mathrm{FR}_{\mathrm{SW}}\) comparing a mechanism’s social welfare to the optimal social welfare among allocations satisfying SI and EF, and defines the price of strategyproofness \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(m)\) under SI, EF, PO, SP. Proves for \(m=3\) that \(2 \le \mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3) \le 3\), leaving the exact value open.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open problem: determine the exact \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)\) for 3-resource Leontief multi-resource allocation under SI, EF, PO, SP, closing the gap between the proven bounds 2 and 3.

sources.title: Beyond dominant resource fairness: Extensions, limitations, and indivisibilities
sources.bibtex:
@article{ParkesProcacciaShah2015BeyondDRF,
  title   = {Beyond dominant resource fairness: Extensions, limitations, and indivisibilities},
  author  = {Parkes, David C. and Procaccia, Ariel D. and Shah, Nisarg},
  journal = {ACM Transactions on Economics and Computation},
  volume  = {3},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {1--22},
  year    = {2015},
  doi     = {10.1145/2723872},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1145/2723872}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows strong impossibility/approximation limits for social welfare under incentive or fairness constraints in multi-resource allocation with Leontief preferences, motivating alternative benchmarks beyond classic approximation to the unconstrained optimum.
sources.connection: Provides the foundational lower-bound framework that Bei–Li–Luo adapt when proving lower bounds on \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(m)\) and in particular when establishing that for \(m=3\) the price of strategyproofness is at least 2 (and at most 3).

sources.title: Dominant resource fairness: Fair allocation of multiple resource types
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces DRF, a strategyproof, envy-free, share-incentive, Pareto-optimal mechanism for Leontief multi-resource allocation by equalizing dominant shares.
sources.connection: Sets the baseline mechanism satisfying SI, EF, PO, SP whose welfare performance (under fair-ratio) is compared against optimal fair allocations; the open problem concerns the best possible fair-ratio achievable under the same axioms for \(m=3\).

sources.title: Fair and Efficient Allocations with Limited Demands
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies fair and efficient allocations under limited demands, extending multi-resource fairness analysis beyond the unbounded Leontief model and exploring algorithmic/axiomatic trade-offs.
sources.connection: Related in spirit (fairness–efficiency tradeoffs under constraints), but does not resolve the exact \(\mathrm{PoSP}_{\mathrm{SW}}(3)\) in the original unbounded Leontief setting; may offer techniques for bounding welfare under additional constraints.
